caveats: a: OEWS is reported at broader NAICS 621100, includes all physician specialties and employer sizes, and measures jobs rather than task hours.
a: AMA adoption is physician-wide and measures use of at least one AI task, not the share of wages technically substitutable or not already automated.
a: Psychiatry has unusually high conversational and relationship content, so exposure differs substantially by medication-management, psychotherapy, and administrative service mix.
rho: The AMA sample spans specialties, employment settings, ownership types, and firm sizes rather than LMM 621112 practices.
rho: Any-use adoption is not implementation depth, autonomous completion, or labor opportunity captured.
rho: Mental-health records, sensitive conversations, state rules, malpractice exposure, and patient trust can slow adoption even when tools are available.
e: No source directly measures the eligible share of LMM-band 621112 firms.
e: The injected count is margin-bridged from establishment-size data and may not align precisely with normalized EBITDA or independent control.
e: NAICS permits practices located in hospitals or HMO medical centers, and statistical firm status does not guarantee a transferable stand-alone operation.
s5: AMA ownership statistics describe physicians, not firms or control transactions, and are not psychiatry-specific.
s5: Movement from private to hospital or corporate practice can occur through employment changes, closures, or affiliations rather than a transferable control sale.
s5: No exact-industry transaction numerator or eligible-firm denominator is available.
q: CMS describes Medicare payment mechanics, not the full commercial, Medicaid, value-based, and cash-pay mix of 621112 practices.
q: No source measures the share of implemented AI gross benefit retained by psychiatry operators.
q: Savings may accrue as reduced after-hours work or added clinical capacity rather than removable payroll or durable margin.
d5: BLS industry output is for all offices of physicians, not mental-health specialists, and chained-dollar output is not a direct constant-quality visit index.
d5: Employment growth is a supply and utilization indicator, not a direct demand measure.
d5: Demand can remain unmet when clinician shortages, network adequacy, coverage losses, or affordability prevent service delivery.
o: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required share of the current 621112 service basket.
o: State scope-of-practice rules, prescribing rules, acuity, payer policy, and practice mix create large variation in substitution risk.
o: The estimate separates direct software displacement from implementation difficulty but cannot fully distinguish software self-service from substitution by nonphysician human providers.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Patients and third-party payers purchase recurring evaluations, psychotherapy, medication management, and care coordination through per-visit fee schedules, negotiated commercial rates, Medicare and Medicaid reimbursement, value-based arrangements, and some cash-pay packages.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal units, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, firms outside the approximate $1-10M normalized EBITDA band, nonphysician mental health practices, outpatient mental health and substance-abuse centers, general physician practices, and operations without transferable clinical and payer relationships.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market private and group practices of MD and DO mental health specialists providing recurring or repeat psychiatry and psychoanalysis services to patients and payers, including evaluation, medication management, psychotherapy, and related clinical administration.

a.bridge: BLS reports employment and wages for the broader Offices of Physicians industry, not wage-weighted task exposure in 621112, while the AMA reports physician-wide AI use by task. The estimate assigns high exposure to scheduling, intake, eligibility checks, coding, documentation, chart summarization, prior authorization, billing, and routine patient messaging; moderate exposure to information synthesis and care-plan drafting; and low exposure to live psychiatric assessment, relationship-dependent therapy, prescribing accountability, crisis judgment, and supervision. The broad physician-office mix may overstate hands-on support roles relative to psychiatry and understate psychiatrist wage weight, while physician-wide AI use may not capture the specialty's conversational documentation opportunity.
caveats: OEWS is reported at broader NAICS 621100, includes all physician specialties and employer sizes, and measures jobs rather than task hours.
AMA adoption is physician-wide and measures use of at least one AI task, not the share of wages technically substitutable or not already automated.
Psychiatry has unusually high conversational and relationship content, so exposure differs substantially by medication-management, psychotherapy, and administrative service mix.
a.change_evidence: A representative 621112 time-and-wage study separating psychiatrist, advanced-practice clinician, clinical support, intake, coding, billing, authorization, scheduling, and management workflows, with current automation and required human-review rates, would replace this proxy.
a.rationale: BLS reports that the broader Offices of Physicians industry had 2.84 million jobs in May 2023: 45.24% in healthcare practitioner and technical occupations, 18.14% in healthcare support, and 26.73% in office and administrative support. The AMA found physician AI use concentrated in documentation of billing codes, charts, and visit notes (21%), creation of care plans or progress notes (20%), research summaries (13%), diagnosis assistance (12%), and chart summaries (12%) in 2024. These functions expose a meaningful but minority wage-weighted share because psychiatrist compensation is high and the core encounter remains judgment- and relationship-intensive.

d5.bridge: BLS projects chained-2017-dollar output for the broader Offices of Physicians industry, not constant-quality demand for 621112. Taking the square root of the 2034-to-2024 output ratio, 893.9/646.9, gives about 1.176 over five years. Broad physician-office output may understate psychiatry because BLS separately cites growing psychiatric-care demand, but it may overstate realizable specialty volume when psychiatrist supply, insurance coverage, and patient affordability constrain access.
caveats: BLS industry output is for all offices of physicians, not mental-health specialists, and chained-dollar output is not a direct constant-quality visit index.
Employment growth is a supply and utilization indicator, not a direct demand measure.
Demand can remain unmet when clinician shortages, network adequacy, coverage losses, or affordability prevent service delivery.
d5.change_evidence: Exact-621112 constant-price revenue and visit volumes by medication management, psychotherapy, and other services, combined with payer enrollment, appointment availability, psychiatrist supply, and BLS projection revisions, would materially tighten the range.
d5.rationale: BLS projects Offices of Physicians output from $646.9 billion in 2024 to $893.9 billion in 2034, a 3.3% compound annual increase, and psychiatrist employment from 27,100 to 28,800, a 6.1% ten-year increase. BLS states that growing demand for psychiatric care and improved mental-health access will support psychiatrist job growth. The base follows the broader real-output path, with a wide specialty adjustment around it.

caveats: No source directly measures the eligible share of LMM-band 621112 firms.
The injected count is margin-bridged from establishment-size data and may not align precisely with normalized EBITDA or independent control.
NAICS permits practices located in hospitals or HMO medical centers, and statistical firm status does not guarantee a transferable stand-alone operation.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of the 214 injected firms using ownership records, payer rosters, claims, and quality-of-earnings interviews to identify independent control, recurring patient and payer revenue, clinician concentration, and normalized EBITDA would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is closely aligned with the default lens because 621112 consists of independent MD or DO psychiatry and psychoanalysis practices serving patients through private or group practices. Most firms in the injected LMM count should therefore provide repeat external clinical services. The discount covers firms controlled by a hospital, HMO, or other corporate parent despite operating at a separate site; practices with nontransferable clinician panels; and entities whose apparent size or normalization does not fit the target band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621112 ($6,501,446,250) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621112 ($10,875,162,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $176,901 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621112); class <5: 10,197 firms, avg revenue/firm $378,035 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $59,730 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,170 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,061,661 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $167,742 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 582 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,103,289 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $332,320 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 374 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,141,842 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $812,411 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 76 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,705,316 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,007,440 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 138 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,068,304 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,906,792 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 214

caveats: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required share of the current 621112 service basket.
State scope-of-practice rules, prescribing rules, acuity, payer policy, and practice mix create large variation in substitution risk.
The estimate separates direct software displacement from implementation difficulty but cannot fully distinguish software self-service from substitution by nonphysician human providers.
o.change_evidence: Claims and patient-flow data tracking services shifted from 621112 practices to self-guided digital products, primary care, nonphysician prescribers, or automated programs, together with regulatory approvals, adverse-event rates, and retention outcomes, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: BLS describes psychiatrists as physicians who diagnose and treat mental illness through counseling, psychoanalysis, hospitalization, and medication, and notes that all states require physicians to be licensed. AI and software can absorb intake, documentation, screening, education, routine follow-up, and some low-acuity self-service, while nurse practitioners and other human providers can substitute for portions of the basket. Complex diagnosis, controlled prescribing, medication risk, crisis assessment, longitudinal accountability, and relationship-dependent therapy preserve a large role for an accountable clinical operator, but not necessarily for every unit of today's psychiatrist-office service.

caveats: CMS describes Medicare payment mechanics, not the full commercial, Medicaid, value-based, and cash-pay mix of 621112 practices.
No source measures the share of implemented AI gross benefit retained by psychiatry operators.
Savings may accrue as reduced after-hours work or added clinical capacity rather than removable payroll or durable margin.
q.change_evidence: Firm-level contracts, claims, clinician compensation plans, and post-implementation cohorts segmented by fee-for-service, capitation, value-based, and cash-pay revenue, with realized labor cost, visit capacity, denial rates, and renewal pricing, would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Psychiatry practices are commonly paid per coded encounter or under negotiated fee schedules rather than reimbursed for actual labor hours. CMS describes Medicare payment amounts by service and calculates them from work, practice-expense, and malpractice RVUs with geographic adjustment. This structure can let a practice retain savings from documentation, billing, and support automation until payer rates or contract terms change, and can create capacity for more visits. Retention is discounted for payer fee updates, value-based sharing, competitive cash-pay pricing, implementation cost, clinician compensation formulas, and the possibility that faster documentation improves workload rather than reducing paid labor.

rho.bridge: The AMA measures whether physicians across specialties used AI for any of 15 tasks in 2024, not the five-year implemented share of exposed opportunity in LMM psychiatry practices. The range starts from rapid physician adoption and task-specific use, then raises implementation over five years for embedded ambient documentation, coding, messaging, and workflow tools while discounting for HIPAA controls, EHR integration, hallucination risk, clinical validation, liability, patient consent, and change management. Incidence of any use overstates current workflow depth, but fast vendor integration and psychiatry's documentation burden may make the 2024 snapshot understate five-year implementation.
caveats: The AMA sample spans specialties, employment settings, ownership types, and firm sizes rather than LMM 621112 practices.
Any-use adoption is not implementation depth, autonomous completion, or labor opportunity captured.
Mental-health records, sensitive conversations, state rules, malpractice exposure, and patient trust can slow adoption even when tools are available.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of independent psychiatry groups measuring production deployment by workflow, clinician adoption, visit coverage, note acceptance without material edits, denial and rework rates, EHR integration, incidents, and labor hours avoided would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: The AMA reported that 66% of surveyed physicians used health AI in 2024, up from 38% in 2023, but individual task adoption remained 10-21%. Physicians identified feedback loops, privacy assurances, workflow integration, training, oversight, accuracy, and liability as adoption needs. Five years permits broad deployment in bounded administrative and documentation workflows, while sensitive clinical decisions and fragmented payer and EHR processes keep implementation below full capture.

s5.bridge: The AMA measures physician-level practice ownership across all specialties, not unique qualifying control transfers among eligible LMM psychiatry firms. The decline in private-practice participation from 60.1% in 2012 to 42.2% in 2024, the increase in hospital ownership, and the rise in private-equity-owned practice participation from 4.5% in 2022 to 6.5% in 2024 show sustained ownership transition. These shares can overstate firm-transfer probability because physician employment can change without a control sale and large acquisitions move many physicians at once; they can understate transfers because sales between physician owners and undisclosed private deals need not change the reported category.
caveats: AMA ownership statistics describe physicians, not firms or control transactions, and are not psychiatry-specific.
Movement from private to hospital or corporate practice can occur through employment changes, closures, or affiliations rather than a transferable control sale.
No exact-industry transaction numerator or eligible-firm denominator is available.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of 621112 acquisitions, retirements with patient-panel transfers, hospital affiliations, and recapitalizations matched to unique eligible firms, with seller EBITDA, physician retention, ownership change, and transaction date, would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: AMA data show a long shift from physician-owned private practice toward hospital and private-equity ownership, with payment pressure, costly resources, and payer administration cited as sale motivations. Psychiatry remains attractive for scarce clinicians and recurring medication-management demand, but clinician retention, professional ownership rules, payer credentialing, and limited specialty-specific deal evidence constrain a higher estimate. A qualifying five-year transfer is therefore material but still a minority outcome.

principal_driver: Recurring psychiatric demand and service-based reimbursement create room to convert documentation and administrative automation into clinician capacity and lower support cost.
principal_weakness: High-value work remains licensed, judgment-intensive, and relationship-dependent, while clinician retention and payer constraints can prevent technical efficiency from becoming transferable cash flow.

caveats: The cited Census manual could not be reopened because the PDF timed out twice; the exact 621112 definition and cross-references therefore remain unaudited in this review.

The a estimate is built from broad physician-office employment shares and physician-wide AI task use rather than a psychiatry-specific wage-weighted task study; its numerical base is judgment despite an explicit proxy bridge and wide range.

The rho base extrapolates five-year implementation depth from physician-wide incidence of any AI use and task-use percentages; the source does not measure autonomous completion, workflow coverage, or labor hours avoided in LMM psychiatry groups.

The s5 proxy uses physician ownership-setting changes as evidence of transfer activity, but those changes are not unique firm control transactions and are neither psychiatry-specific nor tied to the eligible-firm denominator.

CMS supports fee-for-service payment mechanics but not the q retention estimate; realized capture could differ materially with payer mix, clinician compensation, implementation cost, and whether time savings create capacity rather than removable payroll.

The d5 proxy applies broad Offices of Physicians real-output growth to psychiatry, while psychiatrist employment and a qualitative demand statement are supporting context rather than an exact constant-quality demand measure.

The lens marks the industry as non-heterogeneous even though medication-management, psychotherapy, ownership, payer, and staffing mixes can materially alter a, rho, q, and o; the packet acknowledges much of this variation in primitive caveats and ranges, so the flag does not alter the base tier or action.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census PDF timed out on both reopening attempts, so its 621112 definition and cross-references could not be independently confirmed in this audit. The affected e and o inputs are already labeled ESTIMATE, and S7 independently supports the cited psychiatrist duties and licensing context used for o.

The May 2023 BLS page confirms 2,842,350 jobs and the cited 45.24%, 18.14%, and 26.73% major-group employment shares for broad NAICS 621100. It does not measure wage-weighted task exposure or exact-industry 621112 workflows, as the packet discloses.

The AMA page confirms 66% any-use adoption in 2024 versus 38% in 2023, the cited 10-21% task-use rates, and the stated privacy, integration, training, oversight, accuracy, and liability concerns. It does not directly support the numerical a or five-year rho estimates for LMM psychiatry practices.

The AMA page confirms private-practice participation fell from 60.1% in 2012 to 42.2% in 2024, hospital ownership increased, private-equity-owned practice participation rose from 4.5% to 6.5% in 2022-2024, and financial and administrative pressures motivated sales. These physician-wide ownership statistics do not directly measure qualifying firm transfers or exact-industry eligibility.

CMS confirms service-code payment amounts and calculation from work, practice-expense, and malpractice RVUs with geographic adjustment. It supports the fee-schedule mechanism but provides no evidence for the estimated share of AI benefit retained by psychiatry operators.

BLS confirms broad Offices of Physicians chained-2017-dollar output of $646.9 billion in 2024 and $893.9 billion in 2034, with 3.3% annual growth. The square-root five-year transformation is reproducible, but the source is not exact-621112 constant-quality demand.

BLS confirms psychiatrist duties, physician licensing, projected psychiatrist employment of 27,100 in 2024 and 28,800 in 2034, and its statement that growing psychiatric-care demand supports job growth. It does not directly quantify exact-industry five-year demand or the operator-required share of the service basket.

run_meta.title: Offices of Physicians, Mental Health Specialists

sources.supports: Psychiatrist duties, licensing and training, projected psychiatrist employment, and BLS statement that growing psychiatric-care demand supports job growth.
